items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.

items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).

items.q: A transistor in a circuit is often used as a:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Switch or amplifier. “Battery” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which shape in a flow chart represents a decision?
items.why: It's Diamond. “Rectangle” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A PWM signal at 25% duty cycle on a 12 V supply gives what average voltage?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
